coredata.dc:title: Development of active hydrogel absorbent pads based on sodium alginate/potassium-doped carbon dots for chilled pork preservation

coredata.dc:description: In this study, potassium-doped carbon dots (K-CDs) were added to sodium alginate (NaAlg) hydrogel to yield high-strength, antibacterial, antioxidant NaAlg hydrogel absorbent pads (NaAlg/K-CDs) to absorb chilled pork exudate during storage. The results revealed that the swelling rates of the NaAlg/K-CDs peaked at 3286.83%, a figure exceeding that of the commercial absorbent pads. Based on the bacterial diversity and richness results, Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) was evidently the dominant spoilage organism during chilled pork storage. On storage day 6, the P. aeruginosa population in the NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel absorbent pad group was 82% of that in the control group. The total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) value of the NaAlg/K-CDs group remained lower than 0.15 mg/g on day 8. The pH, TVB-N, total viable count (TVC), cooking loss, and volatile odour values of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel absorbent pad group were significantly lower than those in other absorbent pad groups. Moreover, the colour change and shear force values of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel absorbent pad group were lower than those in the control and wood fiber paper absorbent pad groups. These results indicate that the shelf life of the NaAlg/K-CDs-packaged chilled pork was extended by 2 days compared with that of the control. Therefore, the NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel absorbent pads potentially inhibited microbial growth and improved the storage quality of chilled pork. This study provides an alternative solution against traditional chilled pork absorbent pads, further promoting the advancement of fresh meat preservation techniques.

head.ce:title: Development of active hydrogel absorbent pads based on sodium alginate/potassium-doped carbon dots for chilled pork preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, potassium-doped carbon dots (K-CDs) were added to sodium alginate (NaAlg) hydrogel to yield high-strength, antibacterial, antioxidant NaAlg hydrogel absorbent pads (NaAlg/K-CDs) to absorb chilled pork exudate during storage. The results revealed that the swelling rates of the NaAlg/K-CDs peaked at 3286.83%, a figure exceeding that of the commercial absorbent pads. Based on the bacterial diversity and richness results, Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) was evidently the dominant spoilage organism during chilled pork storage. On storage day 6, the P. aeruginosa population in the NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel absorbent pad group was 82% of that in the control group. The total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) value of the NaAlg/K-CDs group remained lower than 0.15 mg/g on day 8. The pH, TVB-N, total viable count (TVC), cooking loss, and volatile odour values of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel absorbent pad group were significantly lower than those in other absorbent pad groups. Moreover, the colour change and shear force values of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel absorbent pad group were lower than those in the control and wood fiber paper absorbent pad groups. These results indicate that the shelf life of the NaAlg/K-CDs-packaged chilled pork was extended by 2 days compared with that of the control. Therefore, the NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel absorbent pads potentially inhibited microbial growth and improved the storage quality of chilled pork. This study provides an alternative solution against traditional chilled pork absorbent pads, further promoting the advancement of fresh meat preservation techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chilled pork is an emerging mainstream product in the meat market owing to its higher nutritional value than that of frozen pork and its superior storability to that of fresh pork (Bonilla et al., 2023; Bonou, Colley, Hauschild, Olsen, & Birkved, 2019). However, its rich dietary protein, essential mineral, and other nutrient content predispose it to microbial contamination, and strict temperature control is required during storage (Liu et al., 2023). Therefore, chilled pork has a relatively short shelf life, resulting in a significant waste of resources (Zeng, Yang, et al., 2023). Moreover, its comparatively high water content allows it to easily exude blood water during storage, thus not only affecting its quality but also promoting microbial growth, further shortening its shelf life (Yuan, Wu, Peng, Xiao, & Chen, 2021). Consequently, adopting appropriate methods to improve the storage quality of chilled pork and extend its shelf life is imperative. The introduction of absorbent pads to packaging is an effective means of addressing these issues (Kenneth, 2017). Commercially available wood fiber paper absorbent pads are widely used to absorb chilled pork exudate. However, the absorption capacity of wood fiber paper absorbent pads is weak, and the absorbed liquid tends to reversely permeate the pork, thus contaminating it (Martin, Adriana, Luigi, & Jorge, 2018; Wang, Yan, Xu, Liu, & Chen, 2022).

In recent years, polymer absorbent pads, such as electrospun nanofiber film, have received considerable attention because of their excellent absorbent properties and outstanding structural designability (Jia et al., 2022). However, the preparation process for this type of absorbent pad is complex, rendering it unsuitable for actual application (Kallem et al., 2021). Interestingly, its desirable mechanical properties, effective encapsulation ability, and excellent swelling performance for hydrogel film make it a potential absorbent pad candidate in the preservation of chilled pork (Far, Naimi-Jamal, Jahanbakhshi, Rostamani, & Keihankhadiv, 2023). In addition, introducing antibacterial and antioxidant agents to absorbent pads may be an alternative means of preserving chilled pork.

Sodium alginate (NaAlg) hydrogel, a native linear polymer, possesses outstanding biocompatibility, excellent absorption capacity, and effective closure, and it is biodegradable and nontoxic (Chalitangkoon, Wongkittisin, & Monvisade, 2020). In addition, NaAlg hydrogel films with antibacterial and antioxidant properties have widely been used in wood dressing, tissue engineering, biomedicine, drug delivery, biomacromolecule immobilisation, active food packaging, and other fields (Fernández, Picouet, & Lloret, 2010). However, the use of active NaAlg hydrogel absorbent pads for chilled pork has rarely been investigated.

Carbon dots (CDs) constitute a novel class of emerging carbon-based nanomaterials (Mathad, Seetharamappa, & Kalanur, 2022). They have been applied to cancer therapy, bioimaging and optical sensors for heavy metals owing to their superior physicochemical properties, such as chemical inertness, stability, non-toxicity, and cost-effectiveness (Cai et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2022). In addition, CDs have emerged as potential fillers in polymers because of their excellent water solubility, antibacterial activity, and antioxidant properties (Magdy, Abdel Hakiem, Belal, & Abdel-Megied, 2021). In recent years, CDs have been applied in edible or biodegradable food packaging films. Ezati et al. prepared CDs and added them to a chitosan/gelatine-based composite film. This composite film has been found to effectively inhibit mold growth on the surface of avocado and improve avocado storage quality. The addition of CDs also improved the ultraviolet-blocking performance of chitosan/gelatine-based film significantly (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, & Rezaei, 2022). Fu et al. prepared a multifunctional bio-nanocomposite film using green-synthesised CDs, and it was used for fresh fish fillets preservation to mitigate nutritional quality loss and extend shelf life of fresh fish fillets (Fu et al., 2022). Based on these, it seems that CDs can be an ideal additive for the preparation of composite film with multiunctions (antibacterial, antioxidant, UV-shielding). Nevertheless, the application of this kind of material on chilled pork preservation has not been reported. In our previous study, potassium-doped (K-CDs) and nitrogen-doped (N-CDs) CDs conferred excellent mechanical, absorbent, antibacterial, and antioxidant properties to NaAlg hydrogel film, rendering it a potentially outstanding candidate for the absorption of chilled pork exudate (Sun et al., 2023). However, studies on this technology and its applications have not yet been reported.

Therefore, this study prepared NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel film and used it as an absorbent pad for chilled pork to improve storage quality and extend shelf life. K-CDs were synthesised using the microwave-assisted synthesis method at 700 W for 180 s, with citric acid and potassium dibasic phosphate as the carbon and potassium sources, respectively. K-CDs were characterised using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Furthermore, K-CDs were added to the NaAlg/CaCO3 polymer dope solution to obtain CDs-modified hydrogel film (NaAlg/K-CDs). The FTIR spectroscopy results, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) findings, swelling rate, water absorption rate, water contact angle (WCA), and antioxidant properties of the NaAlg/K-CDs and NaAlg/gluconic acid δ-lactone (GDL) absorbent pads were determined. Thereafter, the absorbent pads were applied to chilled pork storage. The bacterial community diversity of chilled pork was analysed during storage. Moreover, the quality of chilled pork during storage was characterised by evaluating the total viable count (TVC), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) colony count, pH, total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) value, thiobarbituric acid reactive substance (TBARS) content, colour, cooking loss, and volatile odour. This study developed a new active hydrogel absorption pad to extend the shelf life of chilled pork, which provides a foundation in preservation of meat and meat products. Thereafter, the prepared NaAlg hydrogel pad exhibits excellent water absorption, absorbs the exudation of chilled pork during storage, which expands the application of CDs, making it possible for CDs to be applied in real food system.

NaAlg (pharmaceutical grade, AR: 98%, M w: 198.11 g/mol; mannuronic/guluronic molar ratio of the NaAlg = 1:1) and GDL were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CaCO3 was supplied by Aladdin, Inc. (Shanghai, China). Methanol (absolute), citric acid (CA ≥ 99%), and K2HPO4 were supplied by R & M chemicals. The outer tenderloin part of the pork and wood fiber paper absorbent pads were purchased from a local market in Harbin, China. All reagents were analytical grade and used as received without any further purification.

K-CDs were synthesised using citric acid and K2HPO4. Citric acid, K2HPO4, and water were mixed in a 5:1:10 ratio and subsequently placed in a microwave oven (Midea M1-L213B, China) at 700 W for 180 s. The heated sample was cooled to 25 °C and removed. Thereafter, the sample was added to 50 mL distilled water and centrifuged at 5000 r/min for 10 min. Finally, it was purified using a 0.45-μm syringe strainer, freeze-dried to a powder in a freeze-dryer, and set aside.

TEM analysis of K-CDs was performed following the method of Gencer, Çeven, and Ünlü (2022). K-CDs were dropped on a copper grid coated with holey carbon film. The solvent was evaporated and CDs microstructure observed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (200 kV with Field Emission, HITACHI HT7800). Notably, a carbon support film had to be applied to the copper mesh.

As outlined by Ezati, Roy, and Rhim (2022), the bonding configuration of the CDs was examined at 25 °C using an FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50; Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany). The FTIR spectra were measured in the wavenumber scope of 400–4000 cm−1 at a scan rate of 64 s.

The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were analysed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F7100; Hitachi, Japan). The excitation wavelength was set at 240–400 nm, and the excitation and emission widths were both 15 nm, with a scan speed of 1000 nm per min (Cai et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterisation of naalg/K-CDs absorbent pad

ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the NaAlg/K-CDs pad, we referred to the method of Sun et al. (2023). First, 0.25 g CaCO3 was dissolved in 100 mL deionized water at 25 °C to acquire a CaCO3 suspension [0.25% (w/v)]. Subsequently, 2 g NaAlg powder [2% (w/v)] and the same amount of glycerine were gradually added to the solution with vigorous stirring for 2 h. Thereafter, 0.6 g K-CDs powder [30% (w/v)] was dissolved in distilled water and subsequently coded the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad. The film-forming solution was promptly cast onto a levelled Teflon™ film-coated glass plate (13 cm × 13 cm) and dried at 25 °C for 2 days. The dried film was peeled off the plate and conditioned at 25 °C and 50% relative humidity for 48 h. For comparison, a neat NaAlg film was prepared using the same procedure, except without CDs addition; however, 0.5 g GDL [5% (w/w)] was added to the neat NaAlg film to promote NaAlg cross-linking, and the resulting pad was coded the NaAlg/GDL absorbent pad. The NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 .

The hydrogel absorbent pad was cropped to 1 × 1 cm2 in preparation for FTIR spectroscopy (Nicolet iS50; Thermo Fisher, Germany) at 25 °C. The FTIR spectra were measured in the wavenumber scope of 400–4000 cm−1 at a scan rate of 64 s (Ezati, Rhim, et al., 2022).
The morphology of the films was evaluated using SEM (S–3400N, Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV, as outlined by Wen, Li, Y, and Su (2023).

The samples’ swelling rates were measured using the method described by Mayachiew et al. (2010). Briefly, each sample was cut into a 10 mm × 30 mm piece, weighed, and placed in a beaker of water for 24 h. Thereafter, the water was removed, and the film was dried with filter paper and weighed. The water swelling ratio was computed using Eq. (1):

The antioxidant activities of the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pads were estimated based on the 2,2-diphenyl-1-pycrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-Azinobis- (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6- sulphonate) (ABTS) radical scavenging rates using the method described by Zareie, Yazdi, and Mortazavi (2019). The DPPH radical scavenging activity was determined at 517 nm using Eq. (3):

The WCAs of the blend and hydrogel films were recorded at 25 °C using a contact angle analyser (Model DMs-401; Kyowa Interface Science Co., Ltd., Tokyo), following the method of Maver, Gradišnik, Smrke, Kleinschek, and Maver (2019). A MilliQ water drop with a volume of 3 μL was added to the surface of the measurements, and the equilibration time was 3 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of pads to chilled pork packaging
Fabrication of pads in chilled pork packaging
ce:section.ce:para: First, the wood fiber paper, NaAlg/GDL hydrogel film, and NaAlg/K-CDs hydrogel film were cut into appropriately sized portions (7 × 7 cm2) and placed on the inside of the tray. Thereafter, fresh pork tenderloin was cut into cuboids (5 × 5 × 3 cm3) and placed on top of each absorbent pad, and the tray was sealed with plastic wrap. The chilled pork without an absorbent pad was designated the blank control group (control), while the chilled pork in a wood fiber paper absorbent pad and NaAlg/GDL hydrogel film were labelled the negative control group. The chilled pork in each treatment group was stored at 4 °C for 10 days and taken at specific time intervals (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 days) for characterisation.

The TVC of the chilled pork was determined based on the approach applied by Wang et al. (2024). Briefly, 10 g chilled pork was chopped and put in 90 mL sterile saline for 30 min and homogenised every 10 min under aseptic conditions. Subsequently, the supernatant was taken for gradient dilution, and plate colony counting was performed at 37 °C for 48 h with plate count agar.

Entire genomic DNA cases were extracted using the OMEGA Soil DNA Kit (M5636-02; Omega Bio-Tek, Norcross, GA, USA) and stored at −20 °C (Bolyen et al., 2018). PCR amplification of the bacterial 16S rRNA gene V3–V4 region was performed using the forward primer 338F (5′-ACTCCTACGGGAGGCAGCA-3′) and reverse primer 806R (5′-GGACTACHVGGGTWTCTAAT-3′). Microbiome bioinformatics were conducted using QIIME2 2019.4, with slight modifications. Sequence data analyses were primarily performed using the QIIME2 and R packages (v3.2.0) (Brandfass & Karlovsky, 2008). Beta diversity analysis was executed to investigate the structural variation of microbial communities across samples using visualised principal coordinate analysis (PCoA). Principal component analysis was also managed based on the genus-level compositional profiles (Ramette, 2007).

After determining bacterial diversity, the colony count of P. aeruginosa in chilled pork was performed following an integral approach described by Li et al. (2013). 25 g chilled pork was chopped with a knife and placed in 225 mL normal saline (0.85% w/v), and the resultant mixture was subsequently homogenised for 1 min. Thereafter, the sample (1 mL) was dispersed on P. aeruginosa Agar Base and incubated at 25 °C for 48 h. The outcomes were recorded as the logarithm (base 10) of colony-forming units (CFUs) [log (CFU/g)]. Concurrently, moulds and yeasts were cultured and counted using Rose Bengal Medium (Demain & Martens, 2017).

Minced pork (10 g) was homogenised in 10 mL distilled water at 6000 r/min for 1 min and placed at 25 °C for 30 min. Thereafter, it was filtered using medium-speed qualitative filter paper. The pH of the supernatant was determined using a pH meter (FiveEasy Plus, METTLER TOLEDO, America). The preparation and assessment of each sample was repeated three times.

The TVB-N values of the pork samples were determined, and semi-micro-nitrogen was estimated using the convey microdiffusion method (Farber & Ferro, 1959; Kulawik, Jamróz, Zając, Guzik, & Tkaczewska, 2019). To assess TVB-N, 2.5 g each sample was homogenised with 100 mL 3 % MgO solution (v/w) and distilled for 10 min in 25 mL 4 % boric acid solution using an automatic Kjeldahl distillation unit (Koreatech, KR-DIS-Auto, Korea). Thereafter, using 0.1 M sulfuric acid, the pH was brought to 4.6, and the acid consumed was recorded. The instrument had to be cleaned in time after the measurement.

The TBARS assay was conducted to evaluate lipid oxidation in chilled pork, following the method developed by Wang et al. (2020). Briefly, the sample (1 g) was grounded with 15 mL solution (75 g kg−1 TBA and 1 g kg−1 ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid). The supernatant was filtered, and the mixture containing 1.0 mL filtrate and 1.0 mL aqueous thiobarbituric acid solution (20 mM) was incubated in a boiling water bath for 20 min. Absorbance was measured at 532 nm, and 1,1,3,3-tetraethoxypropane was used to establish the standard calibration curve. The TBARS value was expressed in mg malondialdehyde (MDA)/kg meat.

The chilled pork was chopped before colour measurements (Nethraa et al., 2023). The lightness (L*), redgreen spectral axis (a*), and yellow-blue spectral axis (b*) of each sample were determined using an automatic handheld colorimeter (ZE6000; NIPPON DENSHOKU, Japan). On completing the measurements, readings were taken, and nine repetitions were performed.

According to the method of Kondjoyan, Oillic, Portanguen, and Gros (2013), 35 g minced sample was weighed into a centrifuge tube. The air bubbles in the minced pork were transferred via centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 5 min. The centrifuged samples were heated at 75 °C for 30 min. Thereafter, the centrifuge tube was inverted in an aluminium box for 1 h. Cooking loss was determined using Eq. (5):
(5)
Cooking
loss
( % )
=
W 0

W i
W O
where W
0 and W
i are the weights (g) of the chilled pork before and after heating, respectively.

The shear force of the chilled pork was measured using a TA.XT Plus C mass spectrometer based on the method of Yin et al. (2021). Briefly, pork pieces were chopped into cubes (7.0 × 7.0 × 6.0 cm3) and boiled in a water bath (75 °C, 30 min). After cooking, all chilled pork was sheared once using a Warner–Bratzler attachment equipped with an electronic testing machine. The central portion of the core length was located under the blade for shearing through the predominant muscle fiber orientation. The crosshead speed was determined at 200 mm/min. The evenness peak shear force value of the five cores was directed at the statistical analyses.

The chilled pork was placed in a headspace-sealed bottle and left at 25 °C for 3 h to equilibrate the volatiles. Measurements were performed using a German PEN3 portable electronic nasal odour analyser, and each sample was run in triplicate parallels. The self-cleaning time, sensor zero-time, sample flow rate, and test time were 100 s, 10 s, 300 L/min, and 90 s, respectively. The volatile substances corresponding to the different sensors were: W1C (aromatic substances), W5S (nitrogen oxides), W3C (nitrogen species, aromatic components), W6S (hydrides), W5C (aromatic olefins, polar compounds), W1S (alkanes), W1W (sulphides), W2S (alcohols, aldehydes, and ketones), W2W (sulphur-containing organics, aromatic components) and W3S (long-chain alkanes) (Bassey et al., 2022).

Correlation and cluster analyses of the variations in the properties and TVB-N values of chilled pork were analysed by calculating Pearson correlation coefficients(Zhang, Chen, Dai, Cui, & Lin, 2023).

Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS software, version 24.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). Significance was determined using the Tukey test, and origin was used to plot the results. Data were obtained in triplicate, and they are expressed as the mean ± standard error.

TEM analysis was conducted to determine CDs size. Fig. 2 (A) shows the TEM images of the prepared hydrogel films. As shown in this figure, K-CDs size was 1 nm with uniform distributionm which was in agreement with that of traditional CDs that were synthesised through microwave assisted synthesis methods before (Yang, Zhao, & Lai, 2023).

To elucidate the structure of the prepared CDs, the FTIR spectra of the K-CDs are obsearved in Fig. 2(B). The characteristic peak for the stretching vibration of the –OH bond appeared at 3478 cm−1, while that at 1715 cm−1 was derived from the stretching vibration of the C═O group. The vibration peak at 1427 cm−1 reflected the stretching vibration of C═C. Citric acid was speculated to gradually carbonise to form a carbon core structure centered on C═C during the process of microwave irradiation (Ma, Jiang, Cao, & Yu, 2020). The vibration peak at 1050 cm−1 was corresponded to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–O–C. These results reveal that the CDs were successfully prepared.

As shown in Fig. 2(C), the fluorescence spectra of the K-CDs became red shift when the excitation wavelength was altered from 240 to 400 nm, and a strong fluorescence emission was observed after excitation at 360 nm. The emission spectra were sensitive to the excitation wavelength. A shift in the emission peaks was observed at longer excitation wavelengths, indicating their excitation-dependent emission behaviour, implying the successful synthesis of K-CDs (Wang et al., 2015).

The functional groups and molecular structures of the NaAlg films were examined using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2(B)). The peaks at 3263 cm−1 and 3257 cm−1 were observed in whole films and corresponded to the hydroxyl stretching in NaAlg (Ezati, Roy, & Rhim, 2022). The bands at 2930 cm−1 and 2928 cm−1 matched the C–H stretching vibrations of the alkane groups in NaAlg (John, Yadav, Kumar, Singh, & Hasan, 2020). The peaks at 1602 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1 corresponded to the C═C stretching vibrations. It is speculated that the carbonization of citric acid to form a carbon core structure centered on C═C is preserved in the NaAlg/K-CDs film. The peak at 1028 cm−1 corresponded to the C–O–C groups, and this is attributable to the successful release of Ca2+ by CDs to crosslink with NaAlg in the hydrogel films (Dabestani, Kadkhodaee, Phillips, & Abbasi, 2018; Sun et al., 2020).

The morphology of the fracture surface of the NaAlg/K-CDs and the NaAlg/GDL absorbent pads is shown in Fig. 3 (A). The NaAlg/K-CDs and the NaAlg/GDL absorbent pads both exhibited a three-dimensional network structure. The pores in NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad were smaller than that in NaAlg/GDL absorbent pad, and the three-dimensional network structure of NaAlg/K-CDs became tighter, indicating its higher water absorption capacity.

The WCAs of the NaAlg/K-CDs and NaAlg/GDL absorbent pads are presented in Fig. 3(B), and they were 43.67° and 32.17°, respectively, confirming their hydrophilic properties. Futhermore, the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad exhibited more hydrophobic surfaces than the NaAlg/GDL absorbent pad, possibly because of the presence of self-hydrophobic K-CDs increasing the free volume of the polymer. Konwar et al. found chitosan–CDs hydrogel films to be more water repellent than chitosan hydrogel films as indicated by their high WCA values (Konwar, Gogoi, Majumdar, & Chowdhury, 2015). Khoshkalampour et al. also found the hydrophilicity of the films after the increase of CDs addition (Khoshkalampour, Ahmadi, Ghasempour, Lim, & Ghorbani, 2023).

Fig. 3(C) presents the swelling ratios of the hydrogel absorbent pads. The NaAlg/K-CDs and NaAlg/GDL absorbent pads maintained the completeness of the gel structure after being immersed in water for 24 h. The swelling rates of the NaAlg/K-CDs and NaAlg/GDL absorbent pads were 3286.83% and 2589.35%, respectively. The increment in swelling rate for NaAlg/K-CDs in oweing to its compact network structure as observed in SEM. This result is attributable to the Ca2+ in the NaAlg/CaCO3 system being released in the presence of acidic CDs and GDL to promote NaAlg cross-linking. However, the swelling rate of the hydrogel absorbent pads significantly exceeded that of the wood fiber paper absorbent pad (50%–1800%) (P < 0.05) (Geng et al., 2021).

Fig. 3(C) displays the water absorption rates of the NaAlg/K-CDs and NaAlg/GDL absorbent pads. The water absorption rate of the NaAlg/GDL absorbent pad was lower than that of the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad (P < 0.05). This was consistent with the swelling rate results. A higher water absorption rate enables more exudate to be absorbed, allowing the quality of the chilled pork to be better maintained during storage.

As evidenced by Fig. 3(D), the DPPH and ABTS radical-scavenging capacities of the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad were 56.11% and 65.01%, respectively. This is attributed to the excellent released capacity of K-CDs in absorbent pad according to our previous study. The DPPH radical scavenging activity of the prepared NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad was 30∼40% higher than that of Perilla leaf oil nanoemulsion/polyvinyl alcohol/gellan gum/citric acid absorbent pad (Wang, Tang, Sun, Liu, & Wang, 2023; Alzahrani, 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of pads in chilled pork packaging

ce:section.ce:para: The TVC of chilled pork after various treatmengts are shown in Fig. 4 (A). Evidently, these TVC significantly increased in all groups (P < 0.05) during the storage. According to Wang et al., the TVC of chilled fresh pork should be 6 log CFU/g (Wang et al., 2024). The TVC of the chilled pork in the absorbent pad was lower than that of the control, suggesting that the absorbent pad was beneficial in inhibiting bacterial growth (P < 0.05). Moreover, the TVC values of the NaAlg/GDL and NaAlg/K-CDs groups were lower than those of the wood fiber paper group owing to the higher absorption rate of hydrogel. The TVC of chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs group was lower than that in the other groups, and it remained below the national standard limit on day 8. This indicates that the shelf life of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad was extended by 2 days compared with that in the control sample (Tang et al., 2013). This was attributed to the fact that the release of active K-CDs inhibited the growth of bacteria in chilled pork. Similarly, Khan et al. prepared a packaging film using green tea-derived CDs and it maintained food quality and extended the shelf life of minced pork (Khan, Ezati, Rhim, Kim, & Molaei, 2023; Alavi, Jabari, & Jabbari, 2021).

The PcoA analysis results of chilled pork are shown in Fig. 4(B). The variance contributions of the first (PC1) and second (PC2) principal components were 69.5% and 12.6%, respectively, explaining 82.1% of the total variation. The chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad on day 6 was closer to the day-0 sample compared with other samples on day 6. Futhermore, the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad on day 10 was closer to the day-0 compared with other samples on day 10. These results imply that the changes in the bacterial community of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad were less pronounced than those of other samples (Lia et al., 2019). The composition and relative abundances of bacteria are displayed in Fig. 4(C). As shown in this figure, P. aeruginosa, Bacillota, and Bacteroidota were the dominant bacterial on day 0. The abundance of Bacillota in all samples increased during storage. Other studies have found Bacillus to act as a probiotic bacterial, maintaining the quality of meat products during storage, thus preventing spoilage of the product (Jafari et al., 2017). P. aeruginosa was the dominant microbial species in all groups, the abundance of which increased with increased storage time. Several studies have suggested that P. aeruginosa is the predominant spoilage microorganism in chilled pork (Bassey et al., 2024). P. aeruginosa potentially catabolises creatine and creatinine in pork under aerobic conditions, secreting numerous hydrolytic enzymes, particularly proteases, thus generating spoilage-related volatile compounds (Bassey et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 4(C), the relative abundance of P. aeruginosa in the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad group was lower than that in the control, wood fiber paper, and NaAlg/GDL absorbent pad groups (P < 0.05). This indicates that K-CDs potentially inhibit the growth of spoilage organisms, thus improving the storage stability of chilled pork. The growth number of P. aeruginosa in chilled pork is shown in Fig. 4(D). As displayed in this figure, the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pads yielded the lowest P. aeruginosa counts, exhibiting consistency with the relative abundance experiment results. Moreover, the number of mold and yeast colony was extremely lower than that of P. aeruginosa. As also can be seen in this figure, mold and yeast colonies counts of chilled pork in prepared NaAlg hydrogel absobent pad was much less than these of the other three treatments, indicating the higher antibacterial property of NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pads. The total yeast and mold populations were above the detection limit (4.20 log CFU/g) on day 6 for control sample, which was not acceptable (Britton, Sarr, & Oliver, 2021). However, the total yeast and mold populations were sitll under the detection limit for the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CD absorbent pad.

The changes in the pH value of chilled pork are shown in Fig. 5 (A). As shown in this figure, the pH value of the chilled pork in all groups gradually increased during storage (P < 0.05), mainly because of the alkaline secondary products formed by the microorganism-mediated breakdown of proteins in the pork during storage (Zhang, Chen, et al., 2023). The pH value of the control reached 6.63 ± 0.05 on day 6 and rose to 6.92 ± 0.08 on day 8, which considerably exceeded that of the standard for fresh meat. The pH value of the chilled pork stored in the NaAlg hydrogel absorbent pads was lower than that of the control and wood fiber paper absorbent pad groups. This was attributed to the strong swelling properties of NaAlg hydrogel film, which potentially absorbs chilled pork exudate and improves the chilled pork's contact environment (Ezati, Bang, & Rhim, 2021). Moreover, the pH value of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad group was lower than that in other groups, possibly because of the antibacterial effect of K-CDs. Noteworthily, the chilled pork stored in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pads maintained freshness until day 8. Therefore, NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pads can extend the shelf life of chilled pork by 2 days compared with the control. Similarly, He et al. prepared absorbent pads with antioxidant activity based on polyvinyl alcohol, agarose, and purple sweet potato anthocyanins, and they extended the shelf life of meat by 24 h (He et al., 2022). Liu et al. encapsulated cinnamaldehyde in an oil-in-water emulsion using Tween 80 as an emulsifier, and this also reduced the pH of chilled pork during storage. The pH value of the blank sample reached 6.21 ± 0.07 on day 5 and the sample with cinnamaldehyde exhibited a slight increase to 6.62 on day 9 (Liu et al., 2023).

The TBARS value potentially reflects the oxidation degree of fat in chilled pork and determines its freshness (Fan, Zhang, Chen, Sun, & Yi, 2014). Fig. 5(B) shows the variations in the TBARS value of chilled pork during storage. Evidently, the TBARS value of chilled pork in all groups gradually increased with storage time (P < 0.05), indicating that the degree of oxidation in chilled pork increased. In addition, the acceptable TBARS value for chilled pork has been established at ≤ 0.6 mg/kg (Fang, Lin, & Warnar, 2018). The TBARS values of fresh pork from different treatment groups did not exceed 0.6 mg/kg, even at the end of storage, and this emanated from the lower fat content of the outer pork loin (Huang, Li, Michael, & Ngadi, 2017). Therefore, the freshness of chilled pork needs to be assessed for other indicators.

Furthermore, the TBARS value of the chilled pork in the wood fiber paper group approximated that in the control group. However, the TBARS value of the NaAlg/K-CDs group was expressively lower than that of the control, wood fiber paper, and NaAlg/GDL groups (P < 0.05). This may be attributed to the fact that the CDs released from the NaAlg/K-CDs film inhibited the growth of spoilage organisms, which in turn reduced the amount of lipases produced by the bacteria, resulting in decreased lipid oxidation. Tao et al. found higher TBARS values to contribute to a decrease in immobilised water and an increase in free water in raw pork with badly drip loss (Tao et al., 2023).

TVB-N is also an important factor when assessing the freshness of chilled pork. The variations in the TVB-N value of chilled pork during storage are shown in Fig. 5(C). The TVB-N value in all treatment groups increased with storage time (P < 0.05), indicating a gradual decrease in pork freshness. This was attributed to the fact that proteins in pork decompose to produce volatile nitrogenous substances, such as ammonia and amines, owing to the existence of enzymes and bacteria during storage. The TVB-N value of the control sample increased to 0.16 ± 0.02 after 6 days. In the NaAlg/K-CDs group, the TVB-N value increased from 0.039 ± 0.03 to 0.12 ± 0.05 from day 0 to day 6. Moreover, the TVB-N value of the NaAlg/K-CDs group was significantly lower than that of the other three groups (P < 0.05); nonetheless, it remained lower than 0.15 mg/g on day 8, implying that the chilled pork in NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pads could still be considered as fresh pork (Tuo et al., 2021). Cui et al. also investigated the performance of mulberry leaf ethanol extract in preserving chilled pork and found that the TVB-N value of chilled pork treated with this extract to be lower than that of the control group, thus prolonging its shelf life. The TVB-N value of the control treatment reached 21.3 ± 0.78 mg/100 g on day 9. Moreover, the TVB-N values of the mulberry leaf ethanol extract in preserving chilled pork were lower than 20 mg/100 g, and remained within the range for fresh meat (Cui et al., 2021). Therefore, the oxidative decomposition of proteins can be restrained by NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pads.

The L*, a*, and b* values of the different samples during storage at 4 °C are shown in Table 1 . As shown in this table, all the chromatic difference values of the entire samples gradually decreased during storage (P > 0.05). The decrement in chromatic difference values in each treatment group occurred in the following order: control > wood fiber paper > NaAlg/GDL > NaAlg/K-CDs. Fig. 6 shows the top and bottom images of chilled pork during storage at 4 °C. The colour of chilled pork in each group gradually deepened during storage, and that of chilled pork in the control group was darker than that in the other groups (Du et al., 2022). The chilled pork in the control group exhibited a dark-brown colour, stressed malodour, slime formation, and extreme juice loss, indicating that the control group was more severely spoiled than the other groups (Xiao et al., 2022). The surface of chilled pork from the control and the wood fiber paper absorbent pad groups began displaying stickiness with the loss of juice on day 6. However, the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad group maintained a favourable appearance on day 8. The changes in the appearance of chilled pork were consistent with the above colour difference results, suggesting that the addition of K-CDs to the NaAlg hydrogel film absorbent pad exhibits promising application potential in extending the shelf life of chilled pork by 2 days.

The cooking loss of chilled pork during storage is shown in Fig. 7 (A). As shown in this figure, cooking loss gradually increased with storage time in all samples (P < 0.05). This was predominantly because the proteins in the pork were decomposed owing to the presence of bacteria, damaging the pork's tissue structure, which in turn decreased moisture retention during the cooking process (Doležalová et al., 2023). The chilled pork in the wood fiber paper group exhibited similar cooking loss to that in the control group. The cooking loss value of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad group was significantly lower than that of the other three groups. This was chiefly because the K-CDs released from the pads inhibited microorganism growth in the chilled pork, which in turn decelerated the degradation of the muscle tissue structure and maximised water retention during the steaming process. Wen et al. introduced fluorescent carbon quantum dots from the natural plant turmeric into a chitosan matrix to prepare a series of active composite food packaging films, which improved the storage quality of chilled pork (Wen et al., 2023).

The shear force values of the chilled pork in the various groups are shown in Fig. 7(B). Evidently, the shear force values of the pork decreased with increasing storage time (P < 0.05). This was mainly because the proteins in the pork were decomposed by enzymes and microorganisms during storage, thus damaging the pork's tissue structure, and gradually loosening the cross-linked structure formed by the proteins (Yin et al., 2021). During the entire storage period, the shear force value of the pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs group significantly exceeded that of the other three groups, and it decreased more gradually. On storage day 10, the shear force value of the NaAlg/K-CDs group was 5.55 N, while that of the control group reached 3.12 N. This might have resulted from the proteins in pork undergoing intense contraction and aggregation during heating, leading to the formation of a densely cross-linked structure, and resulting in the hardening of its texture and increased hardness (Jin & Kim, 2023). However, the NaAlg/K-CDs maintained the pork's moisture, enabling it to retain a better tissue structure and allowing the proteins to form a denser cross-linked structure.

The electronic nose is a bionic olfactory analysis technology that can rapidly and accurately evaluate the volatile odour information of chilled pork from various groups (Cheng et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 8 (A), the response values of the W2S, W1W, W1S, W6S, and W5S sensors considerably altered in all treatment groups as the storage time reached day 6 and 10 compared with those on day 0 d (P < 0.05). This suggests that the chilled pork gradually underwent spoilage during storage, possibly because of bacterial growth, resulting in the formation of CO2, H2S, alkanes, and volatile salt nitrogen (Bassey et al., 2022). However, the change in the response value of the control group was the largest in the above sensors. Furthermore, the changes in the response values of the wood fiber absorbent paper and NaAlg/GDL groups slightly decreased, and that of the NaAlg/K-CDs group was the smallest in the above sensors. This indicates that the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad can effectively inhibit the change in the volatile odour of chilled pork and delay its spoilage.

Fig. 8(B) shows the correlation of the volatile odours among the various treatment groups based on PCA. The variance contributions of PC1 and PC2 were 74.5% and 17.9% with a cumulative sum of 92.4% (>85%), respectively. The volatile odours of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/GDL and NaAlg/K-CDs treatment groups were closer to that of fresh pork on day 6, and the samples were still in a fresh state. The volatile odours of the chilled pork in the NaAlg/GDL and NaAlg/K-CDs treatment groups were similar with relatively consistent PC1 values on day 0; nevertheless, they deviated from those of the control group versus the wood fiber paper group and correlated with the volatile odours corresponding to the W1W, W1S, and W6S sensors versus the W5S sensors (Yang et al., 2023). However, the volatile odours of the chilled pork were similar on day 10, and all the treatment groups exhibited rancidity and spoilage.

Correlation matrixes were found to better illustrate the relationship between the property indicators and quality of chilled pork as store time progressed. As shown in Fig. 9 (A), the TBARS value negatively correlated with structural indicators, such as the L*, a*, b*, cooking loss, and shear force values (r = −0.18, −0.37, −0.39, −0.26, and −0.21, respectively). This is consistent with the fact that none of the TBARS values exceeded 0.6 mg/kg. Moreover, the TVB-N values exhibited significantly positive correlation with to the cooking loss, and shear force values (r = 0.88*** and 0.95****, respectively, P < 0.01). Furthermore, the correlation coefficient between TVB-N and structural indicators exceeded that of the other chemical indicators, such as pH, TVC, and P. aeruginosa counts, during storage at 4 °C. Therefore, TVB-N potentially reflects the degree of spoilage of chilled pork during storage (Tuo et al., 2021). Zeng et al. demonstrated that the shelf life of chilled meat may be prolonged using a composite film containing eugenol by determining the TVB-N value (Zeng, Yang, et al., 2023).

Therefore, the quality of chilled pork was categorised according to storage time, with the TVB-N value as a criterion, as shown in Fig. 9(B). Each treatment group was assigned to a category 4 days before the storage period. This indicates that the chilled pork successively began to corrupt from day 6. On day 6, the chilled pork in the NaAlg/K-CDs group displayed a similar colour to on days 0–4, indicating that it was not corrupt on day 6.

This study evaluated the application effect of antibacterial hydrogel film as an absorbent pad in chilled pork tray packaging. The NaAlg/K-CD absorbent pad was found to absorb exudate from chilled pork during storage. The results revealed that the swelling rates of the NaAlg/K-CDs and NaAlg/GDL absorbent pads were 3286.83% and 2589.35%, respectively. In addition, the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad exhibited substantial oxidation resistance. The P. aeruginosa emerged as the dominant strain during chilled pork storage. Furthermore, the NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad effectively inhibited the growth of P. aeruginosa, mold and yeast, proving that it possesses a certain degreed of antibacterial ability. Moreover, correlation analysis demonstrated that TVB-N exhibited an extremely significant correlation and was a crucial indicator for evaluating the quality of chilled pork. The NaAlg/K-CDs absorbent pad developed in this experiment extended the shelf-life of tray-packed chilled pork by 2 days. Investigating antibacterial hydrogel absorbent pads is crucial for prolonging the quality of chilled pork during storage, and K-CDs can synergise with other substances to enhance the pads’ antimicrobial properties. Therefore, future research should focus on enhancing antimicrobial ability of absorbent pads by combining K-CDs with other antimicrobial agents.

Pengyuan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yingao Zhu: Investigation. Lingyu Yin: Investigation. Baohua Kong: Investigation. Xiufang Xia: Conceptualization. Qian Liu: Funding acquisition. Hui Wang: Validation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
